title: Gothic Revival in India: How Bombay Became a Gothic Masterpiece
excerpt: No other city in India has the profusion of neo-Gothic architecture than Bombay. Discover how the British Empire's favourite style was stripped of religious iconography and reimagined on Indian soil.
body_html:
Architecture through the years has been the language through which cultural changes can be recorded and visualised. Gothic revival became the tool for societal change. The role of buildings and structural style became more highly regarded and was recognised as an important part of culture and its ideals.

Empire and Architecture

Neo-Gothic architecture could be understood as an idiomatic progression that operated across material and social spaces. Gothic Revival was the fashionable form of architecture across the British Empire. It evoked continuity, stability, wealth, religious authority and traditions.

Gilbert Scott, the greatest exponent of neo-Gothic architecture of the time, was approached to design the Senate Hall, Library and Clock Tower for the University of Bombay. His designs were an eclectic mix of Gothic styles across Europe, adapted to Bombay's tropical climate with the ornament secularised.

Bombay's Unique Gothic Language

No other city in India has the profusion of the neo-Gothic than Bombay. It was serendipitous that the development of Gothic in Britain coincided with its extensive use in Bombay. Here, it was particularly suited for adaptation — despite describing a nationalist, Christian identity back home, in Bombay it would be stripped of all religious iconography and 'secularised' without losing its essential soaring spirit.

Neo-Gothic buildings in late 19th century Bombay evolved with regional inputs in their secularised iconic schemes — local birds and animals, monkeys, mongoose, peacocks and parrots all nestling nonchalantly in Gothic florets and acanthus leaves, thanks mostly to John Lockwood Kipling at the Bombay School of Art. Such incremental adaptations took the Gothic style to the brink of Indianisation.

The Architects of Bombay Gothic

General J.A. Fuller built the David Sassoon Library and the Goculdas Tejpal Hospital. George Molecey designed the Bombay Telegraph Office. Fredrick Stevens in 1888 created the other great synthesis — the headquarters for the Great Indian Peninsular Railway, Victoria Terminus. These architects and their craftsmen gave Bombay a unique architectural identity that continues to inspire us at Bombay Gothic today.
